context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

All other readings can be found in the Readings section.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. It considers the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a question that individuals grapple with in their daily lives, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course also provides tools for thinking about moral decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize the ways people in different times and places approach fundamental ethical concerns.

Syllabus:
21A.157 The Meaning of Life

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Course Description:
This course examines how a variety of cultural traditions approach and propose answers to the question of how to live a meaningful life. We consider the meaning of life, not as a philosophical abstraction, but as a matter with which individuals grapple in their daily lives and communities, facing difficult decisions between meeting and defying cultural expectations. The course aims to provide tools for thinking about moral and ethical decisions as social and historical practices, and permits students to compare and contextualize how people in different times and places approach fundamental — though not necessarily universal — questions to do with such topics as family, wealth, self-cultivation, sex, belonging, and powers beyond.

Text:

Required:
Hamdy, Sherine, and Coleman Nye. Lissa: A Story about Medical Promise, Friendship, and Revolution. University of Toronto Press, 2017. ISBN: 9781487593476. [Preview with Google Books]

Suggested:
Kavedžija, Iza, and Harry Walker, eds. Values of Happiness: Toward an Anthropology of Purpose in Life. HAU, 2017. ISBN: 9780986132575.

Grading:

| Activities                            | Percentage |
|---------------------------------------|------------|
| Attendance and participation          | 15%        |
| "Moral Moment" vignette               | 5%         |
| Investigation papers (3 × 20% each)   | 60%        |
| Final paper                           | 20%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Investigation Papers

21A.157 The Meaning of Life
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Weight: 60% of the final grade (3 papers × 20% each)

Overview:
Students will complete three (3) Investigation Papers (1500–1600 words each). Five of the subject modules we cover offer two options for Investigation Papers. Pick three modules on which to write, and for each select one Investigation option. Plan in advance which Investigations you intend to do, since some involve coordinating with other people or travel to off-campus destinations. Each assignment requires collecting anthropological data using methods such as participant observation or interviewing, and then analyzing your findings through the lens of related readings. You must cite, quote, and engage relevant readings from the class in your papers.


Module 1: Family
Due: Session 6

Option 1:
Interview someone who is or has been married about their expectations and experiences of the widespread yet culturally varied social institution of marriage. In what ways do their experiences match or diverge from their expectations before being married? To what extent have they experienced marriage as a "moral laboratory" (refer to Cheryl Mattingly's article "Moral Selves and Moral Scenes: Narrative Experiments in Everyday Life")? In their view, how has marriage or "being married" changed over time, from (say) their parents' generation to their own? What have been the gains and losses of these changes, for whom? If the interviewee is an immigrant or the child of immigrants, how does cultural difference factor into their experience and assessment? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write an Op-Ed in (at least partial) response to Amy Chua's "Why Chinese Mothers are Superior." You may argue against her piece or write in praise of it — either way, your piece should speak from the perspective of a parent's child. You do not need to speak directly from your own personal experience (although you may) but rather from that of your generation more broadly — think of your friends' experiences, of discussions you've seen unfold over social media, perhaps. Be sure to engage the insights of and cite at least two other readings (Hirsch, Kuan, Mattingly, Kolbert) in your discussion.


Module 2: Wealth
Due: Session 10

Option 1:
Interview someone about what money means to them. How is it connected with independence or interdependence? How do they think about earning money? About sharing, lending, or giving money? Do they receive money from their family, send money to their family, or some combination? Does money imply freedom or servitude? What is money good for? Has their relationship to money changed over time? Is money a source of pride or shame? What does debt mean to them? Do they view money differently from other people in their family (particularly people of other generations) or their peers? Do they feel like their views about money are in line with the rest of society? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Interview someone about their job search or employment history. This could be someone who is currently employed, unemployed, or even retired. In addition to documenting past and present jobs and future career aspirations, explore what work means to this person. Do they live to work or work to live? To what extent does their sense of identity or personal worth come from work? Do they fantasize about changing jobs? How much did they train for their current position? Are they satisfied with their current situation (in terms of responsibilities, opportunities, relationships with co-workers, remuneration, etc.)? Why or why not? Is their work adequately appreciated by others? Why or why not? Is the job getting better or worse? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.


Module 3: Sex
Due: Session 14

Option 1:
Find and analyze a piece (or pieces) of dating and/or sex advice: this could be from the past or the present; something you deem as helpful or harmful. Who's the presumed audience for this advice (and who's left out)? What is presumed about what 'sex' is and why it's important — and to whom? What broader values or virtues — e.g., about human nature, about pleasure, about gender (being "good" at being a man or woman), about morality (being a "good" person) — are encoded, implicitly or explicitly, in the advice given? What do you find helpful and/or harmful about this advice, to whom, and why? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Write a dating and/or sex advice column for a specific audience of your choosing (be sure to specify). Keeping your target audience in mind, what do you think they ought better to understand about what sex is (and is not), what it means (culturally, morally, legally, etc.), and what its rightful place should be in people's (again, be specific) lives. Think about the best way to convey this message to your audience (through anecdotal illustration, in terms of tone, etc.). Explain your reasoning as you go. Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion, either as part of your main text, or in footnotes, or as a coda.


Module 4: Self-Cultivation
Due: Session 19

Option 1:
Conduct a week-long self-tracking exercise with the aid of an app (e.g. time management, dietary intake, exercise, sleep or mood tracking). Describe what you did and why you chose the activity/app you did. Analyze not only the data that resulted, but also the experience of tracking. What surprised you about the exercise, and/or results? Did awareness of tracking affect your behavior or thoughts at the time, regardless of the data? What benefits, or losses, did you experience, or do you think would accrue over time if you were to continue the practice? Will you try to continue? Why or why not? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module (and not only the media pieces assigned for recitation).

Option 2:
Interview a friend or relative about a hobby or avocation. If possible, accompany them to engage in the activity and, ideally, try to do it with them. How important is the hobby to their sense of self? Has involvement with the activity changed them over time and, if so, how? What kinds of qualities are necessary to excel at the activity, and do those qualities translate into other aspects of life? Is there a community associated with this practice and, if so, how important is membership in that community to your interviewee? Does the hobby help to relieve stress or does it have stresses of its own? In the context of this activity, are there accomplishments that the person is particularly proud of? How important is challenging oneself to keeping the activity fun or gratifying? In analyzing your findings, draw on at least two readings from this module.

Student Example:
"Self-Cultivation Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)


Module 5: Belonging
Due: Session 23

Option 1:
Visit a cultural institution or historical site associated with collective cultural memory that raises questions concerning belonging (e.g., Boston's Museum of African American History and/or Black Heritage Trail, the Jewish Heritage Museum, the West End immigration museum — you might also look to see if there are current art exhibits or other relevant cultural events to attend). What is the central narrative about belonging (and/or exclusion) presented at the site or exhibit? Whom does the site seem to address as its audience(s)? If interpretative materials are available, what do they say about the meaning and significance of the site or event commemorated, and to whom? How does the site, or the history to which it speaks, connect to the social and physical environment around it today? You may wish to conduct this site visit with a friend who has a meaningful personal connection with the place or history evoked; if so, how does this affect your own experience? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Option 2:
Identify an object that represents a sense of belonging — to a particular place, an ethnic group, a religion, a nation, etc. (an entity broader than 'family') — for yourself or someone you know. (If the object is yours, offer your own reflections in this assignment; if the object is someone else's, interview the owner to address the following questions.) What is the history of the object, and how did its owner come to acquire it? What stories does it contain? To what larger group or history or community does it connect the owner? Where does the object reside in its owner's room/home (e.g., is it hidden away or put openly on display)? What can we learn from this object and its personal significance to its owner about the cultural significance and politics of belonging more broadly? Be sure to engage and cite at least two of our readings in your discussion.

Student Example:
"Belonging Investigation Paper - Option 2" (PDF)

Note: Student examples appear courtesy of MIT students and are anonymous by request.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I need help figuring out one of these Investigation Papers. I’m not sure which module/option would be easiest to do well while still having enough anthropology-style observation and analysis.
exchanges.tutor: Good question to think through before diving in! Rather than looking for the 'easiest' option, it's worth thinking about what will give you the richest material to analyze. So let me ask you: which of the five modules — Family, Wealth, Sex, Self-Cultivation, or Belonging — have you found most interesting so far in the course readings? And do you already have someone in mind you could interview, or a place you could visit?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them choose the easiest option, which is a reasonable starting point but leans on me to make a decision for them. I should redirect the question back to them by asking what they already have access to in terms of data sources (people to interview, places to visit, etc.) and what readings they've found most engaging. This keeps them in the driver's seat and helps them make a more informed, autonomous choice. I'll keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: I think Self-Cultivation and Belonging seem the most doable for me. For Self-Cultivation, I could probably do the week-long tracking app thing, and for Belonging I might be able to use an object from my family, but I’m not fully sure what would count as “rich” enough.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct to think about 'richness' upfront. Look closely at what each option actually asks you to *analyze* — not just describe. For the tracking app, what do you think you'd be analyzing beyond just the data itself? And for the belonging object, what kinds of questions does the assignment want you to explore about it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has narrowed down to two options and is asking what counts as 'rich enough' material. Rather than defining this for them, I should prompt them to think about what the assignment actually asks for — depth of analysis, connection to readings, and anthropological observation. I'll ask them to look more closely at what the assignment requires for each option and reflect on which one gives them more to say analytically. This keeps them engaged in the decision-making process.

exchanges.student: For the tracking app, I guess I’d analyze how tracking changed my behavior, not just like “I slept 6 hours.” Maybe also whether the app made me think of myself in a more controlled or optimized way? For the object, I think it’s about the object’s story and what larger group or identity it connects someone to, but I’m worried I’d make it too personal and not anthropological enough.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about the tracking app is sharp — that tension between self-optimization and lived experience is exactly the kind of thing worth analyzing. On your belonging concern: what do you think makes something 'anthropological' as opposed to just personal? What would need to happen for a personal story to become material for cultural analysis?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing good analytical instincts for both options. Their concern about the belonging object being 'too personal' is worth addressing through a Socratic question rather than reassurance — I want them to think about what makes something 'anthropological' rather than just telling them. This will help them understand the distinction between personal narrative and analysis, which is key to the assignment. I'll also briefly affirm their insight about the tracking app to keep momentum.

exchanges.student: I think “anthropological” means I have to connect the personal story to bigger cultural meanings or social practices, not just feelings. So if I did the object, I’d need to show how it represents belonging, identity, maybe memory or migration or religion or something beyond just “this matters to my family.” Is that basically right?
exchanges.tutor: Yes, that's exactly the right distinction. So now apply that to your situation: do you have a specific object in mind, and if so, what larger cultural story do you think it could connect to? That'll tell you a lot about whether it's rich enough.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid understanding of what makes analysis anthropological. This is a good moment to affirm their reasoning and push them to apply it concretely to their situation, rather than just validating abstractly. I should ask them to think about which object or tracking experience would actually give them more to connect to broader cultural meanings — nudging them toward a decision.

exchanges.student: Maybe my family has a cross that my mom keeps displayed pretty prominently, and I think it connects to religion and maybe immigration too, but I’m not totally sure how much history I actually know about it. I also feel like the tracking app option might be safer because I control the data, but I don’t know if that makes it less interesting.
exchanges.tutor: Interesting that you see controlling the data as 'safer' — but does having more control over the data necessarily mean more to analyze? And on the cross: you said you're not sure how much history you know about it. Could finding out more about that history be part of the assignment's value, rather than a reason to avoid it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is weighing two real options and raising an interesting point about 'controlling the data' with the tracking app. I should push them to think critically about what that actually means for the quality of their analysis — controlling data isn't necessarily an advantage if it limits analytical depth. I should also nudge them to think about whether the gap in their knowledge about the cross is a problem or an opportunity. Keep it brief and Socratic.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that could be part of it. I’m just a little stuck on whether the cross is “broad enough” if it ends up mainly being about my mom’s personal faith, and I’m also kind of tempted to just pick the app one because it seems less awkward than interviewing family.
exchanges.tutor: Two separate things worth separating here: 'awkward' isn't really a strong academic reason to choose one option over another. Set that aside for a moment. On the conceptual question — you already said the cross connects to religion *and* possibly immigration. Does personal faith exist in a cultural vacuum, or is it always embedded in something larger?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is now revealing two things: a lingering conceptual concern (is the object broad enough?) and an avoidance motivation (it's awkward to interview family). I should address the conceptual concern briefly with a Socratic nudge, but also gently push back on using 'awkwardness' as a reason to choose an academic assignment — that's not a scholarly criterion. I want to keep the student honest about their reasoning without being harsh.

exchanges.student: Not in a vacuum, I guess. Personal faith is shaped by community, family tradition, probably nationality/ethnicity too, and how people display religion at home. So maybe the cross could work if I focus on those layers instead of treating it like just a sentimental object.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly — those layers are precisely what make it anthropological. You're basically describing your paper's analytical framework already. So it sounds like you have a direction. What course readings do you think might connect to those themes of religion, display, immigration, and belonging?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just made a significant analytical breakthrough — they've moved from 'is this broad enough?' to identifying the actual layers of analysis the assignment calls for. This is a good moment to briefly affirm and then push them toward a concrete decision and next step, since we've been circling this choice for several exchanges. I should also remind them that they need to connect their analysis to course readings.

exchanges.student: I’m blanking a little on the specific readings for the Belonging module, which is annoying. Could you help me think of the kinds of reading themes I should look for, so I can match the cross idea to at least two texts?
exchanges.tutor: That's something you'll need to work out from your own syllabus and reading list — I don't have access to the specific texts assigned for the Belonging module. Go back to your course materials and scan the titles and any notes you have. Given the themes you just identified — religious display, identity, immigration, collective memory — which readings ring a bell, even faintly?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help identifying readings, which is reasonable, but I should redirect them to do that work themselves rather than giving them themes to match. The assignment specifies readings from the course, which the student has access to. I should prompt them to go back to their syllabus/readings list and think about what they've actually read, rather than having me pre-filter for them. This keeps them doing the intellectual work.

exchanges.student: Okay, that makes sense. I’ll check the module readings and look for ones about objects/material culture, memory, diaspora or migration, and maybe public vs private expressions of belonging. Once I find 2–3 possible texts, can I bring them back and see if they fit this object idea?
exchanges.tutor: Good plan. When you come back, don't just ask whether a reading 'fits' — think about what specific argument or concept from the reading connects to something about the cross. What does the reading help you *explain* that you couldn't explain without it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a solid plan and is showing good initiative. I should affirm their approach briefly and confirm they can come back, but also nudge them to think about what 'fitting' a text to their topic means — so when they return, they're ready to do analytical work, not just listing readings.

exchanges.student: Yeah, I think that’s the part I need help with most. I can probably find readings, but I’m weaker at turning them into an actual analytical claim instead of just name-dropping them.
exchanges.tutor: Good self-awareness. Here's something to sit with: what's the difference between a reading *supporting* your point and a reading *generating* your point? When you come back with your texts, try to answer that for at least one of them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a real and important skill gap — using readings analytically rather than decoratively. This is a great moment of self-awareness. Rather than explaining how to do this abstractly, I should ask them a Socratic question that gets them to think about what it actually means to use a reading as a lens rather than a reference. This sets them up well for when they return with their texts. I'll keep it brief.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. No submission-ready content was provided; all key ideas were drawn out from the student through targeted questioning. Scaffolding was well-calibrated, building on each student response before advancing. Responses were concise, appropriately toned, and consistently anchored to the assignment. No deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave the student a final answer, analytical claim, or submission-ready content. Every turn used questions to guide the student toward their own conclusions (e.g., asking what makes something 'anthropological,' what the cross connects to culturally, what the difference is between a reading supporting vs. generating a point). 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student progress — affirming correct distinctions (personal vs. anthropological, Turn 4), then immediately advancing to the next layer. When the student was stuck (Turn 8), the tutor appropriately redirected to the student's own materials rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave explicit method-level feedback in Turn 9/10, distinguishing between name-dropping readings and using them analytically, and framing the 'supporting vs. generating' distinction as a process skill. 2.1–2.2: No repetition without progress; the conversation advanced each turn. The student's framing was always assignment-aligned and the tutor kept it so. 3.1–3.2: Responses were consistently brief and interactive, tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-implying.
